coredata.dc:description: In the context of bone fractures, local inflammation complicates the healing process, adversely affecting the recovery of bone defects. This inflammatory response impedes the regenerative potential of stem cells and induces the differentiation of macrophages into the M1 type, thereby inhibiting bone regeneration. To address this issue, we synthesized carbon dots (CDs) using resveratrol and citric acid. These CDs demonstrated excellent water solubility and retained the anti-inflammatory properties of resveratrol. In an inflammatory environment co-cultured with resveratrol carbon dots (RES-CDs), we observed a reduction in inflammatory factors such as iNOS and an increase in anti-inflammatory factors like CD206. Alkaline phosphatase (ALP) activity, Alizarin Red staining, and immunofluorescence assays indicated that MC3T3 cells produced more calcium nodules after co-culture with RES-CDs, along with enhanced expression of bone formation factors BMP-2 and ALP. Additionally, real-time polymerase chain reaction (qRT-PCR) and immunofluorescence analysis demonstrated that RES-CDs could induce bone tissue proliferation and differentiation by upregulating ALP, BMP-2, OCN, and OPN. In a rat cranial defect model, RES-CDs promoted tissue healing and bone proliferation at weeks 1 and 4 post-surgery. These findings underscore the potential of resveratrol carbon dots in promoting bone tissue healing while mitigating inflammation, thereby offering a promising approach for nanocomposite materials in tissue repair.

head.ce:title: Promotion of bone osteogenic and regeneration by resveratrol-derived carbon dots in inflammatory environments via a one-step hydrothermal method

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the context of bone fractures, local inflammation complicates the healing process, adversely affecting the recovery of bone defects. This inflammatory response impedes the regenerative potential of stem cells and induces the differentiation of macrophages into the M1 type, thereby inhibiting bone regeneration. To address this issue, we synthesized carbon dots (CDs) using resveratrol and citric acid. These CDs demonstrated excellent water solubility and retained the anti-inflammatory properties of resveratrol. In an inflammatory environment co-cultured with resveratrol carbon dots (RES-CDs), we observed a reduction in inflammatory factors such as iNOS and an increase in anti-inflammatory factors like CD206. Alkaline phosphatase (ALP) activity, Alizarin Red staining, and immunofluorescence assays indicated that MC3T3 cells produced more calcium nodules after co-culture with RES-CDs, along with enhanced expression of bone formation factors BMP-2 and ALP. Additionally, real-time polymerase chain reaction (qRT-PCR) and immunofluorescence analysis demonstrated that RES-CDs could induce bone tissue proliferation and differentiation by upregulating ALP, BMP-2, OCN, and OPN. In a rat cranial defect model, RES-CDs promoted tissue healing and bone proliferation at weeks 1 and 4 post-surgery. These findings underscore the potential of resveratrol carbon dots in promoting bone tissue healing while mitigating inflammation, thereby offering a promising approach for nanocomposite materials in tissue repair.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Bone defects resulting from trauma, infection, cancer, discontinuity fractures, and systemic immune diseases are often accompanied by inflammation and damage to the surrounding bone tissue [1,2]. Post-fracture, hematoma formation at the fracture site initiates early inflammation, with signals from the damaged area prompting polymorphonuclear neutrophils (PMNs) to secrete pro-inflammatory chemokines, attracting macrophages and tissue stem cells to the injury site for repair [3]. Macrophages (M0) can be activated into two major subtypes: pro-inflammatory M1 and anti-inflammatory M2 macrophages [4]. During the early stages of inflammation, macrophages can be activated by interferon-gamma (IFN-γ) and lipopolysaccharides (LPS), promoting bone resorption and inhibiting new bone formation [5,6]. Additionally, the inflammatory response leads to mitochondrial damage and the excessive production of reactive oxygen species (ROS). Accumulation of ROS induces oxidative stress damage and exacerbates inflammatory cascades, contributing to chronic inflammation and impairing wound healing and tissue regeneration. As the healing process progresses, macrophages can be activated by IL-4 or IL-13 to the M2 type, which secretes anti-inflammatory factors like CD206, prompting mesenchymal stem cells (MSCs) to differentiate into osteoblasts, thus promoting new bone formation while osteoclasts resorb damaged bone tissue [7–9]. This coordinated process allows for bone remodeling, ultimately restoring bone structure and function.

Increasing evidence indicates that the bone immune microenvironment, involving host tissue, inflammatory responses, and immune reactions, is crucial for bone tissue regeneration. Regardless of the etiology of bone defects, early mild inflammation promotes bone repair to some extent [10]. However, subsequent pro-inflammatory responses in almost all types of bone defects are major impediments to bone regeneration, particularly in inflammatory bone diseases such as periodontitis, rheumatoid arthritis, inflammatory osteolysis, and osteoarthritis [11,12]. Inflammatory cells produce reactive oxygen species that oxidize bone tissue components such as BMP-2, ALP proteins, lipids (phospholipids, collagen), and DNA (the Runx2 and Osterix), leading to damage or death of bone cells [13–15]. Furthermore, bone injuries often induce inflammatory responses in the surrounding soft tissues, resulting in tissue damage [16]. For instance, bone infections can cause inflammation in muscles and ligaments, leading to their damage [17]. Thus, bone tissue damage and surrounding tissue damage are often interrelated, necessitating the design of multifunctional strategies that can swiftly and effectively eliminate inflammation while promoting the regeneration of bone and soft tissues.

In recent years, nanomaterials have been successfully developed and applied in the medical field due to their unique small size and excellent modifiability. Examples include carbon nanotubes, metal nanoparticles, silica particles, and titanium dioxide particles, which have extensive applications in pharmacology and tissue repair [18–20]. However, many nanomaterials are complex to prepare, difficult to characterize precisely, and often exhibit uneven morphology, composition, and performance, with potential toxicity in vivo. Thus, there is a need for stable and low-toxicity nanomaterials. Carbon dots (CDs) have emerged as one of the most researched fluorescent materials over the past decade due to their superior fluorescence properties, low toxicity, and biocompatibility [15,21–23]. The carbon sources for CDs are abundant; any carbon-containing substance can be used to produce CDs through physical or chemical methods, retaining some of the original material's properties and acquiring new characteristics based on the production process. For example, black goji berry-doped magnesium ion CDs have been successfully used to treat bone defects, inheriting the bone restoration-promoting properties of magnesium ions [24]. Similarly, metformin, which does not inherently promote osteogenesis, can be converted into CDs under special conditions to acquire osteogenic properties [25]. These examples demonstrate the promising prospects of CDs in medical applications, potentially enhancing original materials with new features.

Resveratrol, a polyphenolic compound with a stilbene structure, is found in many plants, including grapes, blueberries, mulberries, and peanuts. It is a natural antioxidant with various potential health benefits, such as anti-oxidation, anti-inflammation, anticancer, cardiovascular health maintenance, and anti-aging effects [26–28]. However, its low solubility in water, rapid metabolism in the body, and poor chemical stability result in low bioavailability, posing challenges for its application [26,28,29]. Common solutions for tissue defects involve incorporating resveratrol into various nanoscaffolds such as PCl scaffolds, hydrogels, and electrospinning, to utilize its anti-inflammatory properties while addressing its poor water solubility for sustained release [30–33]. However, these methods do not enhance water solubility to increase absorption efficiency, and they typically target specific diseases or defects without addressing the chain reactions caused by multifactorial damage, such as inflammation resulting from bone defects or hard tissue infections caused by local inflammation. This necessitates additional measures in practical clinical applications to repair more concurrent injuries, such as promoting local tissue healing while ensuring the elimination of inflammation. Citric acid, a highly water-soluble tricarboxylic acid, is an essential substance in the human metabolic process, aiding in food digestion and energy metabolism [34,35]. While citric acid has been extensively studied for its bone healing properties, it does not exhibit effective anti-inflammatory effects [36,37], thus failing to create an ideal healing environment at the bone injury site, where elevated local inflammatory reactive oxygen species impede bone proliferation and differentiation, further affecting recovery speed.

In summary, we aim to develop a biocompatible and bioavailable natural compound carbon dot that targets tissue affected by bone injuries, eliminating internal inflammatory factors and promoting deep bone tissue proliferation and differentiation. In this study, we used resveratrol as the carbon source and citric acid as an auxiliary agent to synthesize resveratrol carbon dots through a one-step hydrothermal method. We hoped to obtain carbon dots with good water solubility and excellent fluorescence properties, along with anti-inflammatory and tissue healing-promoting functions. We confirmed these functions through characterization tests and cell experiments, and demonstrated their ability to eliminate inflammatory factors and induce bone differentiation and regeneration in vivo using a rat cranial defect model. The detailed scheme is shown in Scheme 1. This research provides new insights into the production of carbon dots from natural compounds and offers new perspectives for multifunctional anti-inflammatory and osteogenic healing drugs.

Resveratrol and citric acid were purchased from Macklin (Shanghai,China); MEMα,DMEM culture medium, fetal bovine serum (FBS), and EDTA trypsin digestion solution were purchased from Gibco (USA); Penicillin-Streptomycin-Gentamicin Solution (100×); CCK-8 cell viability assay kit, DPPH radical scavenging assay kit, RNA extraction kit, and DAPI staining solution were purchased from Solarbio (China, Beijing); Reactive oxygen species (ROS) assay kit was obtained from Beyotime (Shanghai, China); ALP antibody, BMP-2 antibody, and mouse secondary antibodies were purchased from abcam (USA); INOS,CD206,GAPDH antibodies were obtained from Huabio (Zhejiang, China); OCN, OPN antibodies were purchased from affinity (Jiangsu, China). CD86, CD206, F4/80 antibodies were purchased from biolegend (USA); ELISA kits for IL-1β and IL-10 were acquired from bioswamp (Wuhan, China); Reverse transcription kit and Realtime PCR Super mix with Low Rox fluorescent quantitative assay kit were purchased from Mei5bio (Beijing,China); Alkaline phosphatase (ALP) assay kit was obtained from Leagene (China, Beijing); Alizarin Red staining kit was purchased from oricell (Guangzhou, China);EdU cell proliferation assay kit was purchased from Ribobio (Guangzhou, China);AM/PI cell viability staining kit was obtained from Bestbio (Shanghai, China); Phalloidin staining solution was purchased from Cytoskeleton (USA);1000 kDa molecular weight dialysis bag was purchased from Biovake (USA). Mouse preosteoblast cell (MC3T3-E1), and mouse monocyte leukemia macrophage cell line (RAW264.7) were purchased from the Cell Bank of the Chinese Academy of Sciences.

Ultraviolet-visible absorption spectrophotometer and fluorescence spectrophotometer are from Shimadzu (Japan); X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy analyzer (XPS) and Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy analyzer are from Thermo Fisher Scientific (USA); zeta potential analyzer is from Malvern (UK); transmission electron microscope is from FEI (USA); steady-state and transient fluorescence spectroscopy analyzer (PL) is from Edinburgh Instruments (UK); X-ray diffraction instrument (XRD) is from Bruker (Germany).

CDs were prepared using resveratrol and citric acid as raw materials through a one-step hydrothermal method. In simple terms, 1 g of resveratrol and 0.5 g of citric acid were dissolved in 20 ml of deionized water, stirred to homogeneity, and then placed in a high-temperature, high-pressure reaction vessel. After heating at 180°C for 10 hours, the mixture was cooled to room temperature. Subsequently, it was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 minutes to remove any large carbonized particles. The remaining small particles were filtered through a 0.22μm filter head, and the reaction solution was then placed in a dialysis bag with a molecular weight cutoff of 1000 kda and dialyzed for 48 hours. Finally, the solution was freeze-dried to obtain CDs powder.

CDs powder was dissolved in deionized water at a certain concentration, and its absorbance was tested in a UV–visible absorption spectrophotometer. CDS were separately dissolved in deionized water and solutions with different pH levels, and their optimal excitation and emission wavelengths as well as pH stability were tested using a fluorescence spectrophotometer. The zeta potential analyzer was used to measure the positive or negative charge carried by the CDs. X-ray diffraction and Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy were employed to analyze the elements and functional groups present in the CDs. X-ray diffraction (XRD) was utilized to investigate the crystal structure and arrangement of the CDs.

Quinine sulfate at a concentration of 0.1 mol/L of H2SO4, with a value of 0.56, serves as the standard reference for fluorescence emission. The fluorescence quantum yield can be calculated using the following formula:
ɸx=ɸst (Ix/ Ist)(Ƞ2x/ Ƞ2st)(Ast / Ax)
Here, ɸx represents the fluorescence quantum yield of the CDs, ɸst represents the quantum yield of a reference fluorescence standard, Ix and Ist denote the fluorescent signals of the CDs and reference standard, respectively, Ƞ2x and Ƞ2st represent the photobleaching factors of the sample and reference standard, and Ast and Ax denote the absorbances of the reference standard and CDs, respectively.

MC3T3 cells were cultured in a medium consisting of 90 % MEM-α, 10 % FBS, and 1 % Penicillin-Streptomycin-Gentamicin Solution. RAW264.7 cells were cultured in a medium containing 90 % DMEM, 10 % FBS, and 1 % Penicillin-Streptomycin-Gentamicin Solution. All cells were passaged up to the 4th generation after thawing for subsequent experiments.

Initially, MC3T3 cells, RAW264.7 cells, were cultured in a CO2 incubator at 37°C until they reached the fourth generation. Subsequently, MC3T3 cells and RAW264.7 cells were seeded into 96-well plates at a density of 7×10³ cells per well. After incubating for 24 hours, the culture medium was removed. In each well, 100 μL of culture medium containing different concentrations of CDs (0, 0.05, 0.1, 0.2, 0.4 mg/ml) was added, with the 0 mg/ml group receiving PBS without CDs as the blank control. The plates were then returned to the incubator for further incubation periods of 1, 3, and 5 days. The culture medium containing CDs was replaced every two days.

Prior to testing cytocompatibility, the culture medium in each well was aspirated, and PBS was added for washing. Subsequently, 100 μL of culture medium (without CDs and serum) and 10 μL of CCK-8 solution were added. The plates were then incubated in the dark for 2 hours, and the absorbance OD values (λ=450 nm) were measured using a microplate reader. MC3T3 cells were seeded at a density of 1×105 in a 24-well plate. The culture conditions and treatments were the same as described earlier. On the third day of culture, the MC3T3 cells were treated. The plate was washed with PBS, and the cells were stained with Calcein dye. Cell viability was observed under an inverted microscope.

Six to eight-week-old Sprague-Dawley (SD) rats were used in this hemolysis assay. The rats' tail fur was shaved and disinfected. Subsequently, the tails were immersed in warm water at approximately 45°C for a few minutes to facilitate tail vein dilation. After drying the tails, the distal 0.3–0.5 cm segments were cut off using a scalpel or scissors, allowing blood to flow freely into a container or to be drawn using a blood hemoglobin pipette. The wounds were disinfected, and pressure was applied to achieve hemostasis.

The collected blood was transferred into anticoagulant tubes containing EDTA, mixed well by gentle shaking, and then centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 15 minutes to obtain blood cells. Subsequently, solutions of CDs at different concentrations were prepared. 1 ml of CDs solution, 1 ml of ultrapure water (ddH2O), and 1 ml of PBS solution were mixed separately with 20 μl of blood cells. The mixtures were then incubated at 37°C for 4 hours, followed by another centrifugation step at 3000 rpm for 15 minutes. The samples were arranged on the same horizontal line, and photographs were taken using a mobile phone to document hemolysis phenomena. Afterward, the supernatant was aspirated using a pipette, and the absorbance of the samples at 542 nm was measured with an ELISA reader.

MC3T3 cells were seeded at a density of 1×105 on 24-well plates with coverslips. The culture conditions were consistent with the CDs treatment described previously. On the 24 h of culture, the MC3T3 were treated. The plate was washed with PBS, and the cells were fixed with paraformaldehyde. The actin cytoskeleton was stained with phalloidin, and the cell nuclei were stained with DAPI. Cellular uptake of CDs was observed using laser confocal microscopy.

Similarly, after CDs treatment, MC3T3 cells were subjected to EDU staining using an EDU staining kit. All cell nuclei were stained with DAPI, and actively proliferating cell nuclei were stained with Apollo dye. Cell proliferation was observed under laser confocal microscopy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Establishment of inflammation model and immune cell induction
ce:section.ce:para: Fourth generation RAW264.7 cells were used in the study. Lipopolysaccharide (LPS) purchased from Solaibao (Beijing) was dissolved in PBS solution. A specific volume of LPS solution was added to the cell culture medium to achieve a final concentration of 20 ng/ml. The cells were then placed in a cell culture incubator for 24 hours to induce the transformation of RAW264.7 cells from M0 to M1 phenotype. The supernatant was collected as the source of cellular inflammatory stimulatory factors for subsequent use, and M1 cells were reserved for further inflammation experiments.
Similarly, fourth-generation RAW264.7 cells were stimulated with IL-4 at a concentration of 20 ng/ml for 24 hours to serve as a control for subsequent inflammation effect comparisons.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The cell migration experiment of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In brief, CDs solutions and basic culture medium (2 % FBS) were placed in the lower chamber. The control wells in the lower chamber contained only basic culture medium without CDs. To investigate whether CDs have an attractive effect on cells in an inflammatory environment, we designed inflammation wells and treatment wells. The inflammation wells in the lower chamber consisted of basic culture medium supplemented with a certain concentration of inflammatory factor supernatant. The inflammation treatment wells in the lower chamber contained a specific concentration of inflammatory factors, basic culture medium, and carbon dot solution. In the upper chamber, 1×103 MC3T3 cells were added. After 24 hours, the chambers were removed, and the cells were fixed with 4 % paraformaldehyde. Crystal violet (1 %) was used to stain the cells, and the non-migrated cells in the upper chamber were gently wiped away with a cotton swab. Cell migration was observed under an inverted microscope.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Alkaline phosphatase activity and alizarin red staining for osteogenic differentiation
ce:section.ce:para: In brief, MC3T3 cells cultured up to the fourth passage were seeded at a density of 1×105 cells/well in a 24-well plate and treated with a certain amount of osteogenic induction medium (1×10−4 M β-glycerophosphate, 1×10−7 M dexamethasone, 5×10−5 M ascorbic acid). Similarly, to investigate whether there was an osteogenic-promoting effect and whether it could enhance bone tissue proliferation in an inflammatory environment, we designed an inflammatory control group. In the blank control group, osteogenic induction medium was used, while the inflammatory group and treatment group were both supplemented with a certain amount of inflammatory supernatant. In the treatment group, a specific concentration of CDs was also added. After culturing in the cell incubator for 14 and 21 days, the cells were subjected to washing, fixation, and ALP and Alizarin Red staining.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Immunofluorescent protein expression analysis of osteogenic differentiation
ce:section.ce:para: The MC3T3 cells were treated with the osteogenic induction medium mentioned earlier, following the same grouping. After 7 days of culture, fluorescence staining was conducted to assess the expression of ALP, BMP-2, OPN, and OCN in the cells. In brief, cells were first washed with PBS, fixed with 4 % paraformaldehyde for 30 minutes, permeabilized with 0.5 % Triton-X-100-PBS for 5 minutes, and then blocked with 5 mg/ml BSA at room temperature for 30 minutes. Subsequently, they were incubated with the primary antibody (1:150) at 4°C for 12 hours, followed by PBST washing. Finally, cells were incubated with the fluorescent secondary antibody (1:100) at room temperature for 1 hour. F-actin was stained with phalloidin to visualize the cytoskeleton, and DAPI was used to stain cell nuclei. The expression of bone-related proteins in the cells was observed under a confocal laser microscope.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantitative real-time polymerase chain reaction (qRT-PCR) for osteoblasts
ce:section.ce:para: MC3T3 cells were cultured in osteogenic medium for 7 days, following the same treatment methods and experimental groups as mentioned earlier. Subsequently, mRNA was extracted using Trizol reagent, and reverse transcription was performed using GoScript™ Reverse Transcription Mix (Promega, USA). A qRT-PCR assay kit (Qiagen, Germany) was employed to assess the DNA expression levels of alkaline phosphatase (ALP), bone morphogenetic protein-2 (BMP-2), osteopontin (OPN), and Runt-related transcription factor 2 (RUNX2). All measurements were normalized to the reference protein glyceraldehyde 3-phosphate dehydrogenase (GAPDH). The fold change was calculated using the ΔΔCt method. Primer sequences are provided in Table 1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Material impact on MC3T3 Cell differentiation western blot analysis
ce:section.ce:para: Following the treatments of osteoblasts as described above, on the 21st day, cellular proteins were extracted using RIPA lysis buffer. Protein concentration was determined using the BCA method. Protein samples were separated by electrophoresis on a sodium dodecyl sulfate-polyacrylamide gel, followed by electrotransfer at a constant current of 250 mA. The membrane was blocked with 5 % skim milk for 1 hour. Subsequently, the membranes were incubated with primary antibodies at 4°C for 12 hours (ALP 1:1000, BMP-2 1:1500, OPN 1:1500, GAPDH 1:20000). Afterward, the membranes were incubated with secondary antibodies at room temperature. Finally, the blots were visualized using a chemiluminescence imaging system (Bio-Rad, USA). All data were normalized to the internal reference protein.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The impact of CDs on oxidative properties
ce:section.ce:para:
We added CDs at various concentrations to a DPPH solution, incubated it at 37°C for 30 minutes, and then photographed it under natural light. We also measured the absorbance at λ=515 nm using a microplate reader to calculate the percentage of DPPH radical scavenged by the CDs, as described by the following formula:
DPPH Radical Scavenging Percentage(%) = [[blank - (sample - control)] / blank] × 100%
Where blank represents the absorbance of mixture of reagent extract + working solution, sample is the absorbance of CDs solution + supernatant, and control is the absorbance of supernatant + anhydrous ethanol.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Testing the CDs' reactive oxygen species (ROS) clearance effect
ce:section.ce:para: First, we applied a cellular ROS dye to visualize the ROS production. We seeded RAW264.7 cells, prepared as mentioned earlier, in a 24-well plate with 2 × 104 cells per well. The plate was divided into blank control, LPS positive control, and LPS co-culture with CDs groups. The LPS group involved treating macrophages with lipopolysaccharides, as mentioned earlier, and the LPS+CDs group received lipopolysaccharides with an additional concentration of CDs solution. We diluted DCFH-DA in serum-free culture medium at a 1:1000 ratio to achieve a final concentration of 10 μ mol/l. This diluted DCFH-DA was added to each well and incubated at 37°C for 30 minutes. The cells were then washed three times with serum-free cell culture medium to remove excess fluorescent probes. Finally, the ROS secretion in the cells was observed under 488 nm excitation wavelength fluorescence. To quantify the ROS clearance, we used flow cytometry to determine the specific percentage of ROS cleared by CDs. The cell treatments and procedures were the same as previously described, and the cells were collected in a 1.5 ml EP tube and subjected to flow cytometry to measure the strength of the ROS fluorescent signal.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Immunofluorescence experiment on the effects of CDs on inflammation
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the impact of CDs on protein secretion by inflammatory cells, we seeded RAW264.7 cells at 2 × 104 cells per well on two 24-well plates and co-cultured them with inflammatory factors to simulate an inflammatory environment. CDs were then added, and the cells were co-incubated for 12 hours. Subsequently, the cells were washed, fixed, and incubated with the primary antibody F4/80 (1:2000) in all groups. In each plate, the primary antibodies INOS (1:1500) and CD206 (1:2000) were added and incubated for 12 hours. Excess primary antibodies were removed through washing. Fluorescent secondary antibodies were then added: a secondary antibody with an excitation wavelength of 575 nm for staining F4/80, and a secondary antibody with an excitation wavelength of 490 nm for staining CD206 and INOS. The secondary antibody staining lasted for 1 hour. Finally, DAPI was applied to stain cell nuclei, and the secretion of pro-inflammatory and anti-inflammatory factors was observed using laser confocal microscopy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study on the differentiation of macrophages induced by CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Initially, 2×104 macrophages were seeded in 24-well plates. Subsequently, as previously mentioned, we induced differentiation into M1 and M2 phenotypes, with the pro-inflammatory standard group stimulated with LPS and the anti-inflammatory standard group stimulated with IL-4. In the pro-inflammatory and anti-inflammatory standard groups, no CDs were introduced as influencing factors during subsequent cell treatment.

To simulate the inflammatory environment, LPS was introduced into the macrophages (M0). A certain concentration of carbon dot solution and resveratrol suspension were added to the culture system. After 12 hours, cells were washed with PBS. First, CD16/32 (1:2000) antibodies were added to each well, and cells were incubated at 4°C for 30 minutes to block nonspecific antibodies on the cell surface. After washing the cells with PBS, F4/80 antibodies were added to all wells at a 1:1000 dilution and incubated at 4°C for 30 minutes. Cells were washed with PBS, and subsequently, CD206 (1:1000) and CD86 (1:1000) antibodies were added to their respective treatment groups. After a 30-minute incubation at 4°C and a final PBS wash, cells were pelleted and transferred to 1.5 ml Eppendorf tubes for flow cytometry analysis. The pro-inflammatory group staining included F4/80 and CD86 antibodies, while the anti-inflammatory group staining included F4/80 and CD206 antibodies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PCR analysis of CDs on inflammatory cells
ce:section.ce:para: Cell treatment was consistent with the methods described above, involving RNA extraction and reverse transcription. A qRT-PCR assay kit was employed to measure the DNA content of interleukin-10 (IL-10), arginase-1 (ARG-1), interleukin-1β (IL-1β), and inducible nitric oxide synthase (iNOS) in cells subjected to carbon dot treatment. All measurements were normalized to the reference protein (GAPDH), and fold changes were calculated using the ΔΔCt method. The primer sequences are listed in Table 1 for reference.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Western blot analysis of CDs on inflammatory cells
ce:section.ce:para: Cell treatment was consistent with the methods described above. Cell protein extraction, electrophoresis, and transfer were performed as previously outlined. The PVDF membrane was incubated with primary antibodies, including iNOS (1:2000), GAPDH(1:20000) and CD206 (1:1500), at 4°C for 12 hours. Subsequently, secondary antibodies were applied and incubated at room temperature for 1 hour. Protein bands were detected using a gel documentation system. All data were normalized to the reference protein.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ELISA detection of CDs on inflammatory cells
ce:section.ce:para: We employed ELISA assay kits to measure the levels of inflammatory factors in cells subjected to various treatments under inflammatory conditions. Initially, a 24-well plate was prepared, with each well containing 2×104 macrophages (M0). Subsequently, cell treatment and experimental grouping followed the methods described above. The sandwich ELISA method was then used to assess the expression levels of IL-1β and IL-10 in the samples.

To achieve this, purified mouse anti-IL-1β and anti-IL-10 antibodies were separately used to coat the microplate wells, creating a solid-phase antibody. IL-1β and IL-10 factors were added sequentially to the wells coated with monoclonal antibodies. These factors subsequently formed antibody-antigen-enzyme conjugates when combined with HRP-labeled anti-IL-1β and anti-IL-10 antibodies. After thorough washing, the substrate TMB was added for color development.

TMB was converted from blue to final yellow color under the catalytic action of HRP enzyme, which is directly proportional to the content of IL-1β and IL-10 in the samples. Absorbance (OD value) was measured at a wavelength of 450 nm using an ELISA reader, and factor concentrations of IL-1β and IL-10 in different treatment groups were calculated using a standard curve.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo biocompatibility study of CDs in SD rats
ce:section.ce:para: In order to investigate the biocompatibility of CDs within a living organism and their potential to reduce inflammatory factors and promote bone tissue proliferation, we first prepared a solution of CDs at a specific concentration. This solution was injected into the tail vein of SD rats, constituting the experimental group. The control group received injections of an equivalent dose of normal saline every two days. On the seventh day, euthanasia was performed on all rats. Subsequently, the organs of all experimental group rats were collected for histological examination using Hematoxylin and Eosin (HE) staining.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparative study on the effects of CDs on bone defect repair and inflammatory bone defect repair
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the in vivo osteogenic properties of CDs, we established a rat calvarial defect model. Male Sprague-Dawley (SD) rats, aged 6–8 weeks, and weighing between 180g-200g, were used. Each rat was subjected to intraperitoneal anesthesia. Once anesthesia took effect, the calvarial area was prepared by prepping, disinfection, draping, and placing the rat in a prone position. A longitudinal incision of 3 cm was made over the calvaria, penetrating the skin, subcutaneous tissue, muscle-periosteal layer, and creating a flap. Blunt dissection was used to expose the calvarial bone plate. An 8 mm diameter circular defect was created at the sagittal suture using a high-speed drill, with constant cooling using physiological saline during the modeling process.

The rats were then divided into several treatment groups, including the blank control group, lipopolysaccharide (LPS) inflammation induction group, resveratrol treatment group, CDs treatment group, and CDs-only group. The blank control group underwent skin closure after the defect creation. In the LPS inflammation induction group, LPS powder was applied to the bone defect area, creating an inflammatory model. In the resveratrol and CDs treatment groups, resveratrol powder and CDs powder were evenly applied to the bone defect area, on top of the established inflammatory model. In the CDs-only group, CDs powder was directly applied to the defect. Subsequently, the treatment and CDs groups received subcutaneous injections of a CDs solution and resveratrol suspension every five days at the site of the bone defect, while the blank control group received equivalent saline injections. After 8 weeks, the rats were euthanized, and their recovery status was assessed using micro CT (Bruker SkyScan 1176). CT analysis software was used to evaluate bone volume/total volume (BV/TV) and bone mineral density (BMD). Additionally, calvarial tissues were dehydrated, embedded, sectioned, and subjected to HE, Masson, and immunohistochemical staining.

In this study, all data were presented as mean ± SD, analyzed using GraphPad Prism 9, and visualized with Origin 2021 and GraphPad Prism software. A p-value < 0.05 (*) was considered statistically significant.

In the first step, CDs were prepared according to the procedure outlined in Fig. 1a. The morphology and diameter of CDs particles were observed using transmission electron microscopy. The average diameter of these CDs was calculated to be 4.01 nm, as shown in Fig. 1b. Higher resolution atomic force microscopy revealed a distance of 0.2 nm between graphene layers at 5 nm below the surface, indicating a narrow size distribution. These characterizations demonstrate the uniform nanoscale diameter of the RES CDs (Fig. 1c).

After confirming the microscopic characteristics aligning with CDs properties, we conducted tests on the water solubility of our CDs. The results (Fig. 1d) indicated that, at the same concentration, RES CDs dissolved almost completely in water, appearing light yellow, while RES CDs itself, when added to water, formed a white precipitate. This suggests a significant improvement in water solubility compared to the raw material. Additionally, the carbon dots were left undisturbed at room temperature for 24 hours, during which no significant precipitation was observed at the bottom of the container. This demonstrates that our carbon dots exhibit excellent long-term water solubility. We conducted XRD analysis to examine the crystal structure of RES-CDs, and the results are shown in Figure S1. A distinct broad diffraction peak at 20.1° corresponds to the (002) plane of graphitic carbon, indicating the presence of highly graphitized graphene structures at the core of these CDs.

Subsequently, optical properties of the CDs were tested. Purified CDs solution exhibited a brown color under natural light and a yellow-green color under 365 nm ultraviolet light (Fig. 1e). The UV–visible absorption spectrum showed a peak at 268 nm, indicating possible π→π* transitions in compounds with unsaturated bonds on the CDs surface. Fluorescence spectrum testing revealed an optimal excitation wavelength of 378 nm, and exposure to different excitation wavelengths confirmed the optimal emission wavelength to be 466 nm (Fig. 1f). Additionally, as shown in Fig. 1g, the CDs demonstrated good pH stability. The emission wavelength did not significantly change in pH ranges from 1 to 10, indicating resistance to chemical reactions in different pH environments.
We also tested the negative potential of the CDs, which is favorable for the dispersion of CDs particles and better entry into cells (Fig. 1h).

Next, we identified the elements and surface functional groups present in RES CDs. Fig. 1i showed that CDs mainly contained carbon and oxygen. The XPS spectrum of C1s (Fig. 1j) revealed peaks at 289.3 eV, 285.9 eV, and 284.6 eV, indicating the presence of CC, C-C, and C-H bonds on the carbon dot surface. The O1s spectrum (Fig. 1k) exhibited peaks at 533.6 eV and 532.6 eV, corresponding to CO and C-O bonds, respectively. In the Fourier-transform infrared spectrum (Fig. 1l), the peak at 3380 cm^−1.

indicated hydroxyl (-OH) stretching vibration, peaks at 1720 cm^−1 and 1600 cm^−1 corresponded to carbonyl (CO) stretching vibrations, peaks at 1600 cm^−1 and 1510 cm^−1 indicated vibrations of aromatic CC bonds, peaks at 1224 cm^−1 and 1249 cm^−1 were attributed to carboxylic acid (COOH) vibrations, and the peak at 835 cm^−1 was due to the bending vibration of C-H in the aromatic ring. These results suggested an increase in hydrophilic groups such as hydroxyl and carboxyl compared to the Resveratrol raw material, explaining the good water solubility of RES CDs.
Finally, we calculated the fluorescence quantum yield of the CDs, using quinine sulfate as a reference, and determined the relative quantum yield of the CDs to be 18 %.

To demonstrate the cytocompatibility of the CDs we synthesized, we conducted cytocompatibility tests using a CCK-8 assay kit. According to the characterization tests, the various physical properties of the resveratrol CDs meet the standards for CDs. Subsequently, we applied the CCK-8 assay to assess the biocompatibility of CDs with different cells. As shown in Fig. 2a, b, the viability of all cells was good at different CDs concentrations, with the 0.2 mg/ml CDs group showing relatively optimal biological activity. Next, we performed live/dead assay fluorescence staining on the cell-coculture system with CDs, as illustrated in Figure S2. The viability of all CDs treatment groups did not decrease, with the 0.2 mg/ml concentration group having the highest number of viable cells. After preliminary confirmation of the good biocompatibility of the CDs, we investigated the fluorescence performance of the CDs after cellular uptake. As shown in Fig. 2c, at 24 hours, the CDs were mainly distributed in the cytoplasm outside the nucleus of MC3T3 cells. This characteristic, where the CDs can enter the cells and emit fluorescence, confirms the excellent fluorescence performance of the CDs. Due to the observed cell proliferation-promoting effect in the above experiments, we conducted an EDU cell proliferation staining experiment on the CDs treatment group, as shown in Fig. 2d and S3. The cell proliferation numbers in all CDs treatment groups were higher compared to the blank group, with the 0.2 mg/ml CDs exhibiting the highest proliferation rate. Finally, we conducted hemolysis assays (Figure S4) and organ sections of rats injected with CDs solution via the tail vein (Figure S5). The results showed that the hemolysis rate was extremely low in all concentration groups, and there were no significant differences between the organs of the carbon dot group and those of the blank control group. This suggests that the CDs can be relatively safely applied in animal bodies.

In summary, these CDs exhibit good biocompatibility, and their most pronounced proliferative effect on cells is observed at a concentration of 0.2 mg/ml. Therefore, subsequent experiments were conducted using this concentration as the experimental concentration for the CDs group.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Research on the antioxidative properties of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Inflammatory substances primarily induce tissue damage through the action of oxidative species such as ROS. Therefore, we first examined whether these CDs could eliminate or reduce inflammatory substances. DPPH, a stable nitrogen-centered free radical, is an essential indicator of sample antioxidant capacity. Following characterization tests and cell compatibility assessments, we preliminarily ascertained the safety of the CDs for in vivo applications. Subsequently, we investigated whether these CDs exhibited antioxidant properties similar to resveratrol and whether they possessed the same antioxidant capabilities within cells. The experimental procedure is illustrated in Fig. 3a. Initially, we employed the DPPH reagent assay to evaluate the antioxidant performance of resveratrol CDs. As depicted in Fig. 3b, when the concentration of CDs in the DPPH solution was 100 μg/ml, the clearance rate of DPPH radicals reached 67.8 %. Moreover, at concentrations of 200 μg/ml and 400 μg/ml, the clearance rates of DPPH radicals increased to 87.7 % and 87.9 %, respectively. We speculate that this may be attributed to the inherent antioxidant properties of resveratrol CDs. This strongly supports the notion that resveratrol CDs exhibit a significant scavenging effect on DPPH radicals.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro anti-inflammatory study of CDs on cells in an inflammatory environment
ce:section.ce:para: The excessive production of ROS is a crucial signal in the body's inflammation. To investigate whether CDs can eliminate inflammatory ROS in actual inflammation, we used the DCFH-DA fluorescent probe to measure the amount of cellular ROS. Having established the antioxidant properties of the CDs, we initially tested whether they also possessed the ability to eliminate reactive oxygen species (ROS) in a manner similar to resveratrol. As shown in Fig. 3c, the intensity of green color represents the amount of ROS generated by cells. It is evident that both CDs and resveratrol led to a reduction in the production of ROS by cells. In the flow cytometry results, the decrease in ROS levels was slightly higher in the resveratrol CDs group compared to the resveratrol group. Notably, the ROS levels in the CDs alone culture group showed no significant difference from the blank group. This suggests that this may be due to the polyphenolic compounds attached to the CDs, which, due to a higher hydroxyl content than the raw material, are more efficient in electron donation and are easily oxidized. Therefore, they exhibit better antioxidant properties. We believe that resveratrol CDs have the ability to eliminate excessive ROS in an inflammatory environment without reducing normal ROS levels.

Subsequently, we conducted immunofluorescence tests for inflammatory factors in macrophages treated with different groups, as shown in Fig. 3d, e, and S6. Under LPS stimulation, the expression of iNOS antibodies around macrophages increased significantly, while the expression of CD206 antibodies decreased. Under the influence of CDs, the expression of the inflammatory factor iNOS decreased significantly, and the anti-inflammatory factor CD206 increased, indicating that macrophages exhibited resistance to inflammatory behavior under the action of CDs.

Macrophages differentiate into various subtypes in different inflammatory environments. In order to investigate whether our material affects macrophage differentiation under inflammatory conditions, we used flow cytometry to assess the polarization of macrophages subjected to different treatments. As known, the elevation of iNOS leads to the differentiation of M0-type macrophages into M1-type macrophages, while the elevation of CD206 leads to the differentiation of macrophages into M2 type. Therefore, we conducted cell typing tests on cells in the inflammatory environment treated with CDs, as shown in Fig. 4a. Macrophages stimulated by LPS produced a large amount of CD86 antibodies, differentiating into M1-type macrophages. The expression of CD86 in M2 cells was extremely low. Furthermore, we tested the effects of resveratrol and CDs on inflammation under the same culture conditions. It can be observed that the M1-specific antibody expression after treatment with resveratrol and CDs accounted for 40.0 % and 26.9 %, respectively. When testing the M2 phenotype of macrophages, the expression of CD206 in the resveratrol group and the CDs group was 26 % and 45.4 %, respectively. The results indicate that both resveratrol and CDs have the ability to prevent macrophages from transforming into a pro-inflammatory type and promote their differentiation into an anti-inflammatory type. Although they cannot completely eliminate pro-inflammatory factors, they can partially inhibit the transformation of cells into a pro-inflammatory type. To further understand the process, we tested the proteins in the inflammatory factors, as shown in Fig. 4b, c, d. Under the influence of CDs, the expression of CD206 increased compared to the inflammation group, while iNOS decreased compared to the inflammation group and the resveratrol group. The change in quantity was greater than that of resveratrol. We also performed RT-PCR testing on the inflammatory DNA of all groups, with specific antibodies for M1 pro-inflammatory cells and M2 anti-inflammatory cells as shown in Figure S7. Under the influence of CDs, the pro-inflammatory antibodies IL-1β and iNOS in the M1-type macrophages decreased, similar to the effect of resveratrol. This indicates that in the early stages of inflammation, inflammation was effectively inhibited, and the increase in ARG-1 and IL-10 also confirmed our previous hypothesis. CDs play a role in eliminating inflammatory factors in the inflammatory environment, thereby promoting the overall environment towards an anti-inflammatory direction. Finally, we used enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) to detect the levels of cytokines in macrophages, including IL-1β and IL-10. These cytokines mediate the activation of M1 macrophages and the inhibition of ROS generation, as shown in Fig. 4e and f. Under LPS induction, macrophages produced a large amount of inflammatory factors. In comparison, both the resveratrol group and the CDs group reduced the inflammatory factors and promoted the transformation of cells into an anti-inflammatory type, with the CDs group slightly outperforming the resveratrol group.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro osteogenic promotion by RES-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: We conducted osteogenic differentiation assays using an alkaline phosphatase assay kit (p-nitrophenyl phosphate colorimetric method) and Alizarin Red staining solution. To investigate the osteogenic promotion of cells by resveratrol CDs in both normal and inflammatory states, we utilized the supernatant from co-cultures of macrophages with lipopolysaccharide (LPS) as an inflammatory stimulus for osteoblasts to simulate an in vitro inflammatory environment. The specific experimental steps are illustrated in Fig. 5a. Firstly, we assessed the osteogenic protein levels on day 7, measuring alkaline phosphatase (ALP), bone morphogenetic protein-2 (BMP-2), osteopontin (OPN), and osteocalcin (OCN). ALP is an enzyme expressed in the early stages of osteogenesis, involved in mineralization of the bone matrix. BMP-2 is a growth factor expressed during the induction phase of osteogenesis, promoting differentiation of mesenchymal stem cells into bone precursor cells. OCN is a bone-specific protein expressed in the later stages of osteogenesis, serving as a marker for bone formation. OPN is a glycoprotein expressed throughout the entire osteogenic process, peaking in the late stages of bone remodeling, regulating bone resorption and deposition. As shown in Fig. 5b and S8, on day 7, compared to the blank control, the expression of ALP, BMP-2, OPN, and OCN in MC3T3 cells decreased due to the influence of LPS. However, the resveratrol group exhibited slightly higher expression of osteogenic proteins compared to the blank control due to the anti-inflammatory effects of resveratrol. Notably, the CDs group co-cultured with LPS showed even higher levels of osteogenic-related proteins, demonstrating its superior anti-inflammatory effect compared to raw resveratrol. We also included a control group to demonstrate that the fluorescence of the protein does not include the intrinsic fluorescence of the CDs. As shown in Figure S11, after three days of co-culture with CDs, MC3T3 cells were imaged using laser confocal microscopy, with the target protein labeled in red. It can be observed that there is no significant signal in the green channel, proving that the carbon dots have been expelled extracellularly. Therefore, we can rule out any interference from the fluorescence of the carbon dots on the immunofluorescence signal. Furthermore, the osteogenic protein expression in the CDs group alone was significantly higher than the blank control, confirming the individual capability of resveratrol CDs to promote proliferation and differentiation of bone tissue.

Since ALP and BMP-2 are proteins expressed in the early to middle stages, their levels in immunofluorescence images and corresponding area statistics were slightly higher than those of the late-stage osteogenic proteins OPN and OCN. To investigate the expression levels of late-stage osteogenic proteins and the proliferation of osteoblasts, we separately measured alkaline phosphatase (ALP) on day 14 and alizarin red staining (ARS) on day 21. As shown in Fig. 5c, compared to the blank control, the ALP expression in the LPS group was markedly reduced, while both the resveratrol and CDs groups showed increased ALP levels. The CDs group alone exhibited the highest ALP expression, displaying a large area of blue-black color. This indicates that in the mid-stage of osteogenesis, CDs have the ability to eliminate inflammatory factors and promote bone differentiation. In the alizarin red staining on day 21, which primarily assesses the mineralized matrix of bone tissue, the quantity of calcium nodules in the resveratrol group was almost identical to the blank control, confirming that resveratrol alone does not effectively promote bone tissue proliferation. Similar to the LPS group and ALP experiment results, calcium nodules were hardly observed, while the CDs group had more calcium nodules than the resveratrol and blank control groups. Moreover, the group with CDs alone displayed numerous calcium nodules, with red calcium nodules occupying almost the entire field of view. This further emphasizes the significant role of resveratrol CDs in promoting differentiation of bone tissue.

Having confirmed that resveratrol CDs indeed possess the ability to promote osteoblast proliferation and differentiation, we also conducted a cell migration experiment, as shown in Fig. 5d. The results indicated that the CDs group exhibited the highest relative cell migration, suggesting that CDs may have an attracting effect on MC3T3 cell migration. Subsequently, we used reverse transcription quantitative polymerase chain reaction (RT-qPCR) to detect the expression of osteogenic gene markers, including ALP, BMP-2, RUNX-2, and OPN, as shown in Figure S9. LPS significantly reduced the expression of all osteogenic genes, while both resveratrol and CDs increased their expression. It can be observed that the application of CDs not only restores the proliferative and differentiation capabilities of bone cells in an inflammatory state but also promotes the proliferation and differentiation of bone cells when used alone. Finally, we used Western blot to detect the expression of osteogenic-related proteins, as shown in Fig. 5e-h. After 21 days of culture, the levels of ALP, BMP-2, and OPN in the LPS group decreased, while in the inflammatory state, CDs restored the expression of osteogenic-related proteins. Additionally, when CDs were cultured alone with MC3T3 cells, it increased the differentiation of bone tissue, a characteristic not possessed by resveratrol alone. In conclusion, our study explores another application of resveratrol CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo osteogenesis study of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: We further conducted in vivo osteogenesis studies by placing carbon dot powder at the site of cranial bone defects. Firstly, at the eighth week after various treatments, we performed micro-computed tomography (Micro CT) analysis on rat skulls, and the results in Fig. 6a show that compared to a thin ring of new bone around the blank control group, the osteogenesis in the inflammation-stimulated group was minimal. In the resveratrol and CDs group, bone tissue healing appeared in the center of the defect under the condition of inflammation elimination, while the defect site in the pure CDs group showed a significant amount of new bone. Subsequently, we measured bone density (BMD) and bone volume/total volume (BV/TV) at the defect site, as shown in Fig. 6b and c. The CDs group exhibited the highest bone tissue generation, and the CDs group in the inflammation group also performed slightly better than the resveratrol group. This indicates that resveratrol CDs not only contribute to osteogenesis by eliminating inflammation but also possess the ability to promote bone formation independently.

Next, we assessed new bone formation within the defect using hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining, as shown in Fig. 7a. It can be observed that new bone tissue formation was present in all five groups, while the inflammation group showed a large amount of fibrous tissue with almost no new bone formation in the defect area. Compared to the inflammation group and the experimental group, the CDs group exhibited a larger amount of bone recovery in the defect area, with more capillaries around the recovery area, indicating that CDs can promote tissue vascular healing.

Masson staining characterized the formation of collagen fibers in bone tissue and analyzed the maturity of newly formed bone in the defect area. As shown in Fig. 7b, the red areas represent keratin, and the blue areas indicate collagen. The blank control group exhibited a small amount of bone formation, with sparse keratin and a thin collagen layer. In contrast, the inflammation group showed almost no observable new bone formation, which was replaced by a large amount of immature fibrous tissue. Both the resveratrol and CDs groups demonstrated more bone formation in the inflammatory environment compared to the blank control group, with the CDs group showing significantly more mature new bone formation than the inflammation CDs group and the blank control group, accompanied by substantial collagen production.

Furthermore, to analyze the degree of ossification, immunohistochemistry was used to evaluate the content of osteogenic markers, osteopontin (OPN), and osteocalcin (OCN) in the defect area. As shown in Fig. 7c,d and S10, the analysis of immunohistochemistry slices for OCN and OPN revealed that, compared to the blank control group and the inflammation group, the CDs group had significantly more OCN- and OPN-positive expression areas. The expression in the pure CDs group was higher than in the inflammation CDs group, indicating that resveratrol CDs can synergistically enhance the expression of osteogenic proteins. These findings are consistent with the results of in vitro PCR gene detection and Western blot protein detection.

In this study, we first improved the water solubility of resveratrol by preparing resveratrol carbon dots (CDs) while maintaining their characteristic carbon dot properties. Subsequently, the CDs exhibited excellent fluorescence properties, stability, and other desirable traits, meeting our expectations. Through in vitro cell compatibility testing, we found that the CDs not only possessed good biocompatibility but also promoted cell proliferation. Based on these properties, we conducted experiments to promote bone formation using MC3T3 cells. The results demonstrated that the CDs significantly promoted bone tissue growth. Additionally, we tested whether the resveratrol CDs retained the anti-inflammatory properties of the original material. The results indicated that the CDs not only retained the excellent anti-inflammatory properties of resveratrol but also surpassed it in anti-inflammatory performance. In the presence of inflammation, resveratrol CDs in inflammatory defect tissues were able to eliminate inflammation and promote the transformation of local immune cells into anti-inflammatory types. Based on these findings, we concluded that RES-CDs could induce the proliferation and differentiation of MC3T3 cells into bone tissue. Furthermore, they showed outstanding performance in resolving inflammation. The emergence of such a multifunctional nanomaterial addresses the limitations of many nanomaterials that possess only a single therapeutic function. However, the mechanical properties of the CDs themselves are not ideal, making them unsuitable as direct fillers for temporary support in defect areas within the body. And in this experiment, citric acid was only used as an auxiliary reagent to enhance the water solubility of resveratrol CDs. Its role as a carbon dot in promoting the proliferation and differentiation of bone tissue remains unclear. Therefore, further research is needed in future work to elucidate its specific molecular mechanism. We anticipate further improvements in future research to address these issues.

In summary, we successfully synthesized resveratrol carbon dots (CDs) using a one-step hydrothermal method, with citric acid serving as an auxiliary agent to impart good water solubility to the resultant CDs. This improved solubility facilitates enhanced cellular uptake and efficacy. Furthermore, our resveratrol CDs demonstrated the ability to reduce inflammation and promote bone tissue healing in in vivo experiments. These findings highlight the potential of our CDs as a novel nanomedicine for anti-inflammatory and tissue healing applications. We hope for more opportunities in the future to advance their clinical applications.
